findings.title: Authentication tokens generated with predictable PRNG
findings.description: Authentication bearer tokens are generated with System.Random, which is not cryptographically secure. Tokens protect authenticated API access and should be generated with a CSPRNG.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker who can observe token generation timing or obtain enough generated tokens can reduce the search space and predict future bearer tokens, then call protected endpoints as the victim while the predicted token is valid.
findings.remediation: Use RandomNumberGenerator.GetBytes or RandomNumberGenerator.GetString with sufficient entropy for session tokens, and compare tokens using fixed-time comparison. Do not use System.Random for security tokens.

findings.title: Generated passwords use predictable PRNG
findings.description: The password generator uses System.Random to choose password characters. Passwords produced by a password manager must use cryptographically secure randomness.
findings.attack_scenario: A user may store or use a password generated by this endpoint. If an attacker can infer generation time or PRNG state, they can predict candidate passwords and attack downstream accounts protected by generated passwords.
findings.remediation: Replace System.Random with System.Security.Cryptography.RandomNumberGenerator for all password character selection. Consider using RandomNumberGenerator.GetInt32 for unbiased index selection.

findings.title: Vault encryption key is derived by base64 truncation instead of a KDF
findings.description: The encryption key for stored vault data is derived by base64-encoding the password, truncating or padding it to 32 characters, and using those bytes as the AES key. This provides no salt, memory hardness, or configurable work factor for offline resistance.
findings.evidence:
string encodedPassword = Convert.ToBase64String(Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(password));
return encodedPassword.Length > 32 ? encodedPassword.Substring(0, 32) : encodedPassword.PadLeft(32);
findings.attack_scenario: If the database is leaked, an attacker can brute-force the user's master password directly against encrypted vault entries because the AES key derivation is cheap and deterministic.
findings.remediation: Derive vault encryption keys with a password KDF such as Argon2id, scrypt, or PBKDF2 with a per-user random salt and strong work factor. Store the salt and KDF parameters with the account.

findings.title: Encrypted vault data lacks authenticated encryption
findings.description: Sensitive account fields are encrypted with Aes.Create and a random IV, but no authentication tag or MAC is stored or verified. Ciphertext tampering cannot be reliably detected before decryption.
findings.attack_scenario: A database attacker can alter encrypted vault fields or tokens. The application will attempt to decrypt modified ciphertext, risking data corruption, chosen-ciphertext behavior, or service disruption because integrity is not checked.
findings.remediation: Use an AEAD mode such as AES-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 and store nonce plus authentication tag. If CBC must remain, add an HMAC over IV and ciphertext and verify it before decryption.

findings.title: Master password policy permits four-character passwords
findings.description: The application allows primary account passwords as short as four characters. This is especially risky because the password is also used to derive the vault encryption key.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker can brute-force weak user passwords online or offline after database compromise, then decrypt the victim's saved credentials because the vault key is derived from the same weak password.
findings.remediation: Require a substantially longer master password or passphrase, such as at least 12-14 characters, block known compromised passwords, and use rate limiting plus strong KDF parameters.

findings.title: Missing Authorization on Favicon Retrieval Endpoint
findings.description: The Get endpoint in FaviconsController is missing authorization, allowing any unauthenticated user to retrieve favicons.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker can access favicon data without authentication, potentially exposing internal resources or enabling enumeration attacks. This could reveal information about the application's structure or internal systems.
findings.remediation: Add the [Authorize] attribute to the Get method in FaviconsController.cs to require authentication before allowing favicon retrieval.

findings.title: Hardcoded MongoDB connection string in development settings
findings.description: The development configuration contains a hardcoded MongoDB connection string. Even though it points at a local Docker host, committed connection settings can expose internal topology and are commonly copied into less-controlled deployments.
findings.attack_scenario: If the development configuration is reused in staging or production, an attacker with access to deployed files can learn database connection details and internal host naming, making follow-on database access or network mapping easier.
findings.remediation: Remove connection strings from committed appsettings files and load them from environment variables, user secrets, or a managed secret store. Keep only non-sensitive placeholders in source control.

findings.title: Insufficient PBKDF2 iteration count
findings.description: Password hashes are derived with only 10,000 PBKDF2 iterations, which is below modern recommendations and reduces the cost of offline password cracking after database compromise.
findings.attack_scenario: An attacker who obtains the PrimaryAccounts collection can run offline guesses against password hashes faster than they could with a stronger work factor, increasing the chance of cracking weak master passwords.
findings.remediation: Increase PBKDF2 iterations to a current calibrated work factor, such as 100,000 or more based on server performance, and store KDF parameters with each hash for future upgrades.

findings.title: Users can create notes on another user's secondary account
findings.description: CreateNote loads the requested SecondaryAccountId and creates a note for it, but does not verify that the secondary account belongs to the authenticated PrimaryAccountId. The source comment also acknowledges the missing ownership validation.
findings.attack_scenario: An authenticated attacker who obtains or guesses a victim secondary account id can create notes attached to the victim's account. The victim may later load corrupted or attacker-controlled encrypted note data, causing integrity loss or denial of service for that account item.
findings.remediation: Before creating the note, load the secondary account and require secondaryAccount.PrimaryAccountId == request.PrimaryAccountId. Return a generic authorization failure when ownership does not match.

findings.title: Insufficient PBKDF2 output length
findings.description: Password hashing derives only 20 bytes from PBKDF2. A 32-byte output is a more appropriate baseline for modern password hash storage and gives stronger margin for future cryptographic use.
findings.attack_scenario: If password hashes are stolen, the shorter derived hash output gives less security margin than modern 256-bit outputs while the low work factor already makes offline attacks cheaper.
findings.remediation: Derive at least 32 bytes from PBKDF2, use an explicit modern hash algorithm such as SHA-256 or SHA-512, and store versioned hash parameters for migration.

findings.title: Deprecated RNGCryptoServiceProvider usage
findings.description: The code uses RNGCryptoServiceProvider to generate password-hash salts. The API is obsolete in modern .NET; RandomNumberGenerator should be used instead.
findings.attack_scenario: This is not immediately exploitable, but continued use of deprecated cryptographic APIs increases maintenance risk and may lead to weaker or unsupported implementations during future framework upgrades.
findings.remediation: Replace RNGCryptoServiceProvider with RandomNumberGenerator.Fill(salt) or RandomNumberGenerator.GetBytes for salt generation.
